## Comparing Estimators {#sec-comparing-estimators}

Suppose several estimators are available for the same parameter $\theta$.

If two estimators are both centered at $\theta$, the estimator with the smaller variance is preferable because its sampling distribution is more concentrated around the true parameter.

A biased estimator can have very small variance while remaining systematically displaced from $\theta$. Variance alone therefore does not measure closeness to the true parameter when bias is present.

## Mean Squared Error {#sec-mean-squared-error}

::: {#def-mean-squared-error}
## Mean squared error

Let $\hat{\theta}$ be an estimator of the parameter $\theta$. The **mean squared error** of $\hat{\theta}$ is

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\mathbb{E}\left[
(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2
\right].
$$

The quantity $\hat{\theta}-\theta$ is the estimation error, and mean squared error is its expected squared magnitude.
:::

If $\hat{\theta}$ is unbiased, then

$$
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta}).
$$

## Bias {#sec-estimator-bias}

::: {#def-estimator-bias}
## Bias

The **bias** of an estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is

$$
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
=
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
-
\theta.
$$
:::

An estimator is unbiased when

$$
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
=
0.
$$

## Variance, Mean Squared Error, and Bias {#sec-mse-bias-variance-decomposition}

Write the estimation error as

$$
\hat{\theta}-\theta
=
\left(
\hat{\theta}-\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
\right)
+
\left(
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]-\theta
\right).
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
&=
\mathbb{E}\left[
(\hat{\theta}-\theta)^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}\left[
\left\{
\left(
\hat{\theta}-\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
\right)
+
\left(
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]-\theta
\right)
\right\}^2
\right] \\
&=
\mathbb{E}\left[
\left(
\hat{\theta}-\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
\right)^2
\right] \\
&\quad
+
2
\left(
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]-\theta
\right)
\mathbb{E}\left[
\hat{\theta}-\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
\right] \\
&\quad
+
\left(
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]-\theta
\right)^2 \\
&=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta})
+
2
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
\left(
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
-
\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}]
\right) \\
&\quad
+
\left(
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
\right)^2 \\
&=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta})
+
\left(
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
\right)^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

::: {#thm-bias-variance-decomposition}
## Bias-variance decomposition

For any estimator $\hat{\theta}$,

$$
\boxed{
\operatorname{MSE}(\hat{\theta})
=
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta})
+
\left(
\operatorname{Bias}(\hat{\theta})
\right)^2
}
$$
:::

Thus mean squared error combines two distinct sources of estimation error:

- variability around the estimator's own mean;
- systematic displacement of that mean from $\theta$.

When comparing estimators and at least one is biased, compare their mean squared errors rather than their variances alone.

## Relative Efficiency {#sec-relative-efficiency}

Suppose $\hat{\theta}_1$ and $\hat{\theta}_2$ are two unbiased estimators of $\theta$.

::: {#def-estimator-efficiency}
## Efficiency

The estimator $\hat{\theta}_1$ is more efficient than $\hat{\theta}_2$ when

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta}_1)
<
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta}_2).
$$
:::

::: {#def-relative-efficiency}
## Relative efficiency

The relative efficiency of $\hat{\theta}_1$ relative to $\hat{\theta}_2$ is

$$
\operatorname{Eff}
\left(
\hat{\theta}_1,
\hat{\theta}_2
\right)
=
\frac{
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta}_2)
}{
\operatorname{Var}(\hat{\theta}_1)
}.
$$
:::

Therefore,

$$
\operatorname{Eff}
\left(
\hat{\theta}_1,
\hat{\theta}_2
\right)
>
1
$$

implies that $\hat{\theta}_1$ is more efficient than $\hat{\theta}_2$.

Similarly,

$$
\operatorname{Eff}
\left(
\hat{\theta}_1,
\hat{\theta}_2
\right)
=
1
$$

means that the two unbiased estimators have equal variance.
